# The First Stars

Long ago, when the sky was endless black velvet, the Night herself grew lonely. She watched the Earth below, where fires flickered and danced, bringing warmth and light to the people. The Night yearned for her own companions.

One evening, she descended to a village and spoke to a young weaver named Aria. "Your fires are beautiful," Night said. "Would you teach me to create light of my own?"

Aria thought carefully. "Fire needs fuel to burn, and you have none in your realm. But I can offer something else." She took her finest silver thread and began to weave, her needle flashing in the firelight. She wove all through the night, creating thousands of tiny knots, each one holding a spark of light from the fire.

When dawn approached, Night gathered the shimmering tapestry and returned to her domain. She scattered the glowing knots across her dark canvas. Each knot became a star, burning eternally without fuel, kept alive by the love and skill woven into every thread.

From that day forward, Night was never lonely, and the people below could always find their way by the light of Aria's gift—the eternal stars.
myths.Agent_2:
# The Moon's Borrowed Light

Long ago, the Moon was born without light of her own. She wandered the night sky in darkness, invisible and lonely, while her brother the Sun blazed brilliantly across the day.

The Moon wept silver tears that fell to Earth as dewdrops. "Why must I live in shadow?" she cried.

The Sun, hearing his sister's sorrow, made her an offer. "I cannot share my fire, for it would consume you," he said. "But each month, I will lend you my light to wear as a gown."

The Moon agreed joyfully. At the month's beginning, the Sun gave her a sliver of light. Each night, he added more, until she wore a full dress of radiance that illuminated the entire Earth below. People gazed up in wonder at her beauty.

But the gown was only borrowed. As the month continued, the Sun slowly took back his light, piece by piece, until the Moon stood naked in darkness once more. Then, the cycle began anew.

This is why the Moon waxes and wanes—forever borrowing her brother's brilliance, shining for a time, then returning to shadow, only to be clothed in light again. She accepted her fate, for even borrowed beauty was better than eternal darkness.

Myth: Long ago, the Moon refused to accept borrowed light. Unlike her sister planets who gleamed with the Sun's reflection, she chose darkness, wrapped in stubborn pride. "I will shine with my own fire, or not at all," she declared.

The Earth below grew fearful. Without moonlight, navigation failed, tides ceased their rhythm, and night creatures lost their way. The oceans sent a delegation of waves to plead with her.

Still, the Moon refused, sitting cold and invisible in her sky throne.

This is why the Moon controls her own cycle—she accepted help, but never surrendered her will.
